text: We present a case of discoid resection of invasive rectosigmoid endometriosis. The incidence of bowel endometriosis ranges from three to thirty-seven percent. Symptoms include painful bowel movements, constipation, diarrhea, alternating constipation and diarrhea, intestinal cramping, nausea and or vomiting, abdominal pain, rectal pain, rectal bleeding, and possibly appendicitis. Ninety percent of the lesions involve the sigmoid or rectal colon. Multidisciplinary laparoscopic treatment of extensive bowel endometriosis is standard of care, and indications for bowel resection include invasion of more than fifty percent of the bowel circumference, multiple nodules or nodules larger than three centimeters. Complications include anastomotic leakages, pelvic abscess, erecto-vaginal fistula, the recurrence rate after bowel resection, can be quite high, and cumulative pregnancy rates after bowel resection are acceptable.

The surgeon inspects the pelvis, revealing the bladder cuff and sigmoid colon. The area of invasive endometriosis is identified and circled. The surgeon palpates to determine the upper and lower margins of the nodule, placing a straight suture through these margins to facilitate later use with a stapling device. The suture is shortened for easier management.

The surgeon advances a circular stapler into the rectum, positioning it to draw the nodule into the stapling device. The stapler is opened, the nodule is brought inside, and then closed to excise the nodule. Pressure is applied posteriorly to ensure proper excision of the anterior wall. After firing the stapler, a sigmoidoscopy is performed to check for bleeding and inspect the anastomosis site. An anastomosis leak test is conducted by clamping the bowel with an atraumatic grasper, filling the pelvis with fluid, and inflating the bowel with air to check for leaks.
The surgeon removes excess fluid from the pelvic cavity, ensuring no residual fluid remains. Any additional procedures are finalized, marking the completion of the surgical operation.

Multidisciplinary minimally invasive laparoscopic excision and treatment of extensive bowel endometriosis is a standard of care and recommended. Discoid resection of the invasive bowel endometriosis nodules can be safely performed depending on the size and location of the lesions.
